facts.claim: Cursor pagination achieves 17x performance improvement compared to offset pagination
At page 50,000 on a 10M row dataset, keyset pagination is 7,889 times faster than offset pagination
Offset pagination query time grows from 0.28ms at offset 0 to 138ms at offset 999,990 on 1M record table
Stripe API cursor pagination uses starting_after and ending_before parameters, mutually exclusive, with limit 1-100
Facebook Graph API cursor expires and should not persist for hours or days; empty/null next_cursor indicates end of results
Composite index required for cursor pagination: CREATE INDEX idx ON table (sort_field DESC, id DESC)
Cursor pagination prevents duplicate/phantom reads during concurrent inserts; offset pagination suffers from row shifting
Twitter API v2 returns next_token that does not expire; multiple requests with same token yield consistent results
Slack API cursor expires within reasonable time; cursor validation returns invalid_cursor error when gibberish or outdated
Contentful moved all content APIs to cursor-based pagination to support large dynamic datasets with stable ordering
Keyset pagination uses compound WHERE clauses: (sort_field, id) > (last_value, last_id) with O(1) complexity
HTTP 206 Partial Content status used for paginated responses returning subset of collection with Content-Range header
Base64 encoding cursors provides opacity; prevents clients from reverse-engineering or manually constructing tokens
Maximum nesting depth for REST resources is 2-3 levels; deeper nesting causes path explosion and URL complexity
OpenAPI x-speakeasy-pagination extension supports offsetLimit, cursor, and url-based pagination configuration
API versioning via path (/v1, /v2) is preferred for public APIs; allows independent evolution and deprecation
Deprecation strategy: announce → coexist → remove with Sunset header and progressive cutover via gateway routing
RFC 8288 Link header provides first, next, last pagination URLs; eliminates client URL construction
Postgres timestamp ordering breaks under concurrent writes; rows can commit out-of-order, requiring composite keys with IDs
Cursor tokens can encode filtering/sorting parameters; parameters locked in cursor and cannot change between pages

examples.implementation: Cursor-based pagination with starting_after/ending_before parameters; has_more flag indicates remaining results; limit 1-100; auto-pagination in client libraries
Cursor pagination with cursor and limit params; response_metadata.next_cursor indicates next page; empty cursor = end; cursor expires within reasonable timeframe
Cursor-based pagination returning next_token in response; up to 100 tweets per request; non-expiring tokens provide consistent results across requests
Cursor pagination with after/before parameters; PHP SDK handles cursor management automatically; best practice: don't pre-fetch large amounts of data
Moved all APIs (CMA, CPA, CDA) to cursor-based pagination; encodes all query parameters in cursor token; stable ordering prevents duplicates in dynamic datasets
Keyset pagination with composite indexes: SELECT * FROM table WHERE (updated_at, id) > ('2025-01-01', 123) ORDER BY updated_at ASC, id ASC LIMIT 20
At page 1000 (offset 20,000) over 1M rows: offset consumed 10x CPU and 14x memory vs cursor-based approach

analogies.analogy: Offset pagination is like counting from the beginning of a book every time you want to resume reading; cursor pagination is like bookmarking where you stopped
Offset pagination discards rows like tearing pages out of a book and throwing them away; cursor pagination jumps directly to the marked position
Composite cursor keys (id, updated_at) are like a two-part address: the timestamp says which day, the ID says which item that day—uniquely identifying your position
Opaque cursors are like encrypted gift cards—you can't read the value without the system decrypting them, preventing tampering and reverse-engineering
Base64-encoded cursors are like license plate covers—they obscure the details from casual inspection while remaining fully functional

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cursor pagination always outperforms offset pagination
common_misconceptions.reality: Offset works fine for small, rarely-changing datasets (e.g., paginated search results with <10k rows). Cursor complexity isn't worth it until data volume or change frequency justifies it.

common_misconceptions.misconception: You can determine end of pagination by checking if results < limit
common_misconceptions.reality: Always check next_cursor/next_page/has_more flags explicitly. Result count alone is unreliable with concurrent writes; cursor absence is the authoritative signal.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cursor pagination works with arbitrary sort orders
common_misconceptions.reality: Requires stable, unchanging sort fields. If sorting by updated_at, rows updated after cursor generation will disappear or reorder. Use composite keys with unique IDs as tiebreakers.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Any field can be the sort key
common_misconceptions.reality: Sort keys must be indexed and stable. Updating a sort key causes items to jump or vanish. Timestamps are unreliable under concurrent writes (rows commit out-of-order). Use surrogate IDs or (timestamp, id) compounds.
Cursors expire (hours/days depending on API). Don't cache cursors for long periods. Slack explicitly warns against persistence; Twitter tokens don't expire but are exceptions.
Opaque cursors are intentionally unreadable to clients. Don't assume base64 encoding means you can parse it; APIs reserve the right to change encoding. Treat as blackbox.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cursor pagination eliminates need for indexes
common_misconceptions.reality: Requires composite indexes on sort columns in the same order. Without indexes, cursor performance collapses to sequential scans.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Offset pagination works fine at 1M+ rows
common_misconceptions.reality: Performance degrades exponentially. OFFSET 1M forces the database to scan and discard 1M rows. At scale (100M+ rows), even OFFSET 10k becomes expensive.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cursor pagination prevents all duplicates/skipped records
common_misconceptions.reality: Stable ordering required. With unstable sort fields or concurrent deletes of items before cursor position, duplicates/skips still occur. Isolation levels matter.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Nested resources can go arbitrary depth
common_misconceptions.reality: Depth >3 causes path explosion, rigid API structure, and maintenance burden. Flatten using query parameters or separate endpoints with explicit IDs after 2-3 nesting levels.
Stateless tokens (base64-encoded, JWT-based cursors) are possible. Server validates token signature, not stored state. Enables horizontal scaling.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Link headers are optional in pagination
common_misconceptions.reality: RFC 8288 Link header is best practice; provides rel=next/prev/first/last with URLs. Reduces client burden of constructing pagination URLs and enables HATEOAS.
